chief_complaint: A 35-year-old female patient presented with a 3-day history of polyuria, polydipsia, and vomiting, leading to a diagnosis of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA).
history: The patient was newly diagnosed with diabetes mellitus (DM) and presented with DKA, characterized by hyperglycemia, metabolic acidosis, and ketosis. She had negative insulin-related autoantibodies and no identifiable infectious etiology. The patient developed Guillain-Barre syndrome (GBS) with atypical features consistent with the acute motor axonal neuropathy (AMAN) variant, and reversible splenial lesion syndrome (RESLES).
exam: Vital signs: temperature 37.2°C, blood pressure 120/80 mmHg, pulse 100 beats per minute, respiratory rate 24 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Physical examination revealed weakness in the lower extremities and decreased deep tendon reflexes.
labs: Laboratory investigations: blood glucose 350 mg/dL, pH 7.1, bicarbonate 10 mmol/L, lactate 4.5 mmol/L, creatine kinase 250 U/L, and white blood cell count 12,000 cells/μL. Electroencephalogram (EEG) showed a reversible splenial lesion syndrome (RESLES).
correct_diagnosis: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) complicated by Guillain-Barre syndrome (GBS) and reversible splenial lesion syndrome (RESLES)
differentials: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) complicated by Guillain-Barre syndrome (GBS) and reversible splenial lesion syndrome (RESLES)
Acute motor axonal neuropathy (AMAN) variant of Guillain-Barre syndrome
Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) with negative insulin-related autoantibodies
Acute kidney injury secondary to diabetic ketoacidosis
